claims.text: The An Lushan Rebellion lasted from 755 to 763 CE; An Lushan (703-757) served as Tang military commissioner for the Hebei frontier before launching his rebellion against the imperial court in 755, ending a fifty-year period of stability (Shao-yun Yang, Late Tang China and the World, 750-907 CE, Cambridge University Press).

By the time the rebellion was quelled in 763, the rich agricultural heartland of the Central Plain lay in ruins and hundreds of thousands had perished; the emperor was forced to flee the capital Chang'an, which was occupied and later devastated (Richard von Glahn, The Economic History of China, Cambridge University Press).

The late eighth-century Tang scholar-official Du You estimated the actual population before the An Lushan Rebellion at 13-14 million households, or about 70 million individuals, well above the officially registered population of 48.9 million in 742 (David A. Graff, 'The Reach of the Military: Tang', Journal of Chinese History, Cambridge University Press).

The Tang dynasty was plagued by internal conflict including the An Lushan Rebellion, culminating in the sacking of the capital Chang'an by the Tibetan Empire in 763 (peer-reviewed study on Tang-dynasty decline, PubMed Central, U.S. National Library of Medicine).

description: A massive revolt against China's Tang dynasty launched in 755 CE by An Lushan (703-757), the military commissioner commanding the empire's Hebei frontier armies, who turned his troops on the imperial court and proclaimed his own dynasty. Rebel forces seized the Tang capitals Luoyang and Chang'an before the rising was finally quelled in 763, leaving the agricultural heartland of the Central Plain in ruins. The rebellion permanently weakened Tang central authority, ended a fifty-year period of imperial stability, and marked one of the great watersheds of Chinese history.

impact.summary: Popular death tolls of 13 to 36 million rest on the collapse of Tang census registers after the war, but modern scholarship attributes most of that register collapse to the breakdown of Tang census-taking and taxation rather than to mortality, and the pre-war registers themselves undercounted: the eighth-century Tang statesman Du You put the true pre-rebellion population near 70 million against a registered 48.9 million in 742. Cambridge University Press's Economic History of China states that by the time the rebellion was quelled in 763 'hundreds of thousands had perished'; because no rigorous overall toll exists, the bounds are left unquantified here rather than repeating disputed census arithmetic.

location.region: Northern China: Hebei, Henan and the Tang capitals Luoyang and Chang'an (modern Xi'an)

name: An Lushan Rebellion
